retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Gingival Overgrowth -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: DIGO is most commonly seen in male children and adolescents, with the most prevalent location being in the anterior gingival tissue. The individual's genotype affects the development of DIGO, [12] and the extent and degree of overgrowth depend on the drug. Phenytoin, cyclosporin, and nifedipine are the most common causes of gingival overgrowth, and phenytoin has the highest prevalence. There is an estimation that 50% of adults treated with phenytoin experience gingival enlargement, 30% with cyclosporin, and 20% with nifedipine. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Gingival Overgrowth -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Drugs are the most common reason behind gingival enlargements. DIGO is an adverse event in patients taking anticonvulsants, immunosuppressants, or calcium channel blockers. This condition is associated with the patient's genetic predisposition and plaque or gingival inflammation. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Gingival Overgrowth -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Differentiate drug-induced gingival overgrowth from other causes of gingival enlargement, such as idiopathic gingival hyperplasia or inflammatory conditions. Screen patients taking anticonvulsants, immunosuppressants, or calcium channel blockers for early signs of drug-induced gingival overgrowth to prevent progression. Implement appropriate diagnostic protocols, including medical history reviews, drug interaction assessments, and oral examinations, to confirm drug-induced gingival overgrowth. Communicate various interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and outcomes in patients with drug-induced gingival overgrowth. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Gingival Overgrowth -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Drug-induced gingival overgrowth (DIGO), also referred to as drug-induced gingival enlargement and previously known as drug-induced gingival hyperplasia, is a side effect of certain drugs in which the gingival tissue is not the intended target organ. The key offending drug classes are anticonvulsants, immunosuppressants, and calcium channel blockers. [1] Gingival overgrowth impedes proper dental hygiene and, apart from the cosmetic damage, causes painful chewing and eating (See Images. Gingival Overgrowth From a Medication and Gingival Overgrowth).

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Gingival Overgrowth -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Drug-induced gingival overgrowth (DIGO) is a condition characterized by an excessive enlargement of the gingival tissues due to the adverse events of certain systemic medications. The most frequently implicated drug classes include anticonvulsants such as phenytoin, immunosuppressants like cyclosporine, and calcium channel blockers, notably nifedipine and amlodipine. DIGO arises when these drugs alter the metabolism of gingival fibroblasts, leading to excessive extracellular matrix deposition and tissue proliferation. This condition typically presents as firm, fibrotic gingival tissue that may obscure the teeth, causing aesthetic and functional challenges, including difficulty in maintaining oral hygiene and an increased risk of periodontal infections. While the severity of DIGO varies based on individual susceptibility and drug dosage, contributing factors such as oral hygiene status and genetic predisposition significantly influence its progression.

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Gingival Overgrowth -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnoses for DIGO include the following: False enlargement of gingival tissue: Pseudo-enlargement of the gingiva caused by an enlarged underlying bony tissue. The gingiva here is normal. Inflammation: Chronically inflamed gingival tissue is red or violaceous, smooth, and tends to bleed to the touch. Familial or hereditary conditions: Examples include familial fibromatosis, idiopathic fibromatosis, gingivomatosis, and hereditary gingival hyperplasia. The gingiva is pink and non-tender, involves the attached gingiva, the gingival margin, and the interdental papillae, and has a firm and leathery consistency. Physiological states: Puberty and pregnancy are associated with gingival enlargement. Scurvy: Vitamin C deficiency can produce very tender bleeding gingiva. Systemic diseases: leukemia, tuberculosis, sarcoidosis, as their diagnosis can be corroborated hematologically.

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Gingival Overgrowth -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This course provides participants with in-depth knowledge about DIGO's etiology, pathophysiology, and clinical manifestations, along with strategies for diagnosis and management. Participants gain insights into recognizing early signs of gingival overgrowth, assessing risk factors, and implementing preventive measures such as optimizing oral hygiene and evaluating alternative drug therapies where possible. The course emphasizes the importance of interprofessional collaboration involving dentists, pharmacists, and other healthcare professionals to create personalized treatment plans. This team-based approach facilitates timely interventions, promotes adherence to care recommendations, and enhances overall patient outcomes by reducing DIGO's functional and aesthetic impact while addressing the underlying systemic conditions requiring medication.

retrieved_docs.title: Excessive Gingival Display -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: A gummy smile is multifactorial in etiology, and more than 1 factor can present simultaneously (see Image . Excessive Gingival Display, Multifactorial in Etiology). Professionals must accurately identify the cause of the condition since the treatment differs according to the etiology. [3] [4] Short upper lip length Hypermobile upper lip Altered passive eruption Gingival hyperplasia Vertical maxillary excess (VME) Dentoalveolar extrusion Vertical Maxillary Excess (VME)

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Gingival Overgrowth -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with DIGO report a past medical history of hypertension, angina, epilepsy, or a recent organ transplant. They typically present with gingival enlargement, pain during mastication, and cosmetic disfigurement 1 to 3 months after starting treatment with 1 of the drugs associated with the disease. [4] The gingival enlargement caused by the drug makes oral hygiene challenging, leading to increased plaque accumulation and secondary inflammation. [15] Not all drug-induced gingival overgrowth lesions are fibrotic, as it was previously believed. According to histological and histomorphometric analyses, phenytoin-induced lesions are the most fibrotic. Cyclosporin-induced lesions are more inflamed and show a low fibrotic component, and nifedipine lesions are mixed. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Gingival Overgrowth -- History and Physical -- Clinical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical findings of DIGO include the following: Firm, painless, nodular enlargement of the interdental papilla, limited to the keratinized portions of the gingiva and extending to the facial and lingual gingival margins. In severe cases, a large fold of hypertrophied gingival tissue is observed covering the crowns. If no secondary inflammation is present, it appears firm and pale pink with minute lobulations, pouting from underneath the gingival margin. This is delineated by a groove of tissue that does not bleed on touch. If secondary inflammation exists, the gingiva appears smooth and red or bluish-red. The enlargement is generalized but usually greater in the anterior regions. Typically, it is not seen in edentulous areas. [15] The gingival overgrowth disappears when teeth are extracted. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Gingival Overgrowth -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The treatment of DIGO aims to alleviate the patient's discomfort, enabling them to do simple acts like eating and chewing pain-free, treat the inflammation, reduce the swelling, and improve the gingiva's cosmetic appearance. The modalities of treatment are medical and surgical. Medical management is the first line of therapy, whereas surgery is reserved for recurrences or cases that persist despite good medical treatment. Discontinuing or changing the medication must be considered. [18] [12] An alternative to phenytoin includes carbamazepine and valproic acid, which have shown a lower rate of gingival enlargement. Diltiazem and verapamil exhibit a lower prevalence of gingival enlargement compared to nifedipine. Cyclosporin substitution is more complicated because there are limited options available; cyclosporin can be substituted by tacrolimus, and azithromycin in combination with cyclosporin has shown a decrease in the severity of DIGO. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Gingival Overgrowth -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Suppose the medication inducing the gingival overgrowth can be changed to a drug that is less associated with this complication. In that case, the gingival tissue may return to normal with adequate plaque control. The gingival enlargement can persist despite drug substitution and good plaque control, and in this case, surgical management to restore normal gingival contours is required. These modalities of treatment, although effective, do not always prevent the recurrence of the enlargement. DIGO recurrence in surgically treated cases may appear as soon as 3 to 6 months after the procedure. In general, the results last for at least 12 months. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Gingival Overgrowth -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Since the condition is entirely drug-induced, patient education about the adverse events of the implicated drugs before starting treatment is warranted, and the importance of good dental hygiene should be emphasized [2] . Drug-induced gingival enlargement has a good prognosis and is generally reversible on stopping or substituting the offending drug. An interdisciplinary approach is needed as the primary clinician, dentist, and healthcare team should adequately address the patient's signs and symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Gingival Overgrowth -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The complications that can manifest with drug-induced gingival overgrowth are as follows: Functional difficulties Esthetic concerns Increased incidence of caries Delayed eruption of teeth Pathological drifting of teeth Prolonged retention of the primary dentition Diastemas and spacing problems Poor plaque control leads to periodontal complications [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Drug-Induced Gingival Overgrowth -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: In DIGO, the pathological changes lie in the connective tissue and not the epithelial cells of the gingiva. There is an excessive accumulation of extracellular matrix-like collagen with varying amounts of inflammatory infiltrates, predominantly plasma cells. Fibroblastic proliferation may not be evident; erratic columns of collagen fibers are seen interspersed with penetrating epithelial ridges. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Excessive Gingival Display -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The periodontal examination includes measuring the width and thickness of the attached gingiva, the clinical and attachment level, the crestal bone level concerning the CEJ, and probing depths. [1] A clinical short tooth can be due to gingivitis, gingival hyperplasia, altered passive eruption, or tooth wear, and examining the periodontal tissues helps identify the etiology. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 170
retrieved_docs.content: adverse‑event reporting and clinical management. Recognizing sodium monofluorophosphate as a fluoride source guides oral‑health preventive strategies, while awareness of the SMQ classification aids clinicians in systematic identification and treatment of functional GI disturbances, underscoring the importance of these entities for patient care and product design.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2849
retrieved_docs.content: Oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي) is clinically associated with nonspecific chest pain and with fatigue (التَّعَب). These findings indicate that patients with oral candidiasis may present not only with typical oral lesions but also with systemic or atypical symptoms such as unexplained chest discomfort and generalized tiredness, underscoring the importance of considering this fungal infection in the differential diagnosis when such nonspecific complaints arise.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1923
retrieved_docs.content: التصلب العصيدي is a medical condition that is associated with ذَبْهَةُ برنزميتال. Clinically, التصلب العصيدي is linked to several findings, including النفخة الانقباضية, حفظ الصحة الروتيني, عرج, and لغط الشريان السباتي. In addition, التصلب العصيدي has a recognized subtype called التصلب العصيدي المعمم, reflecting a more generalized form of the disease. These associations are important for recognizing the clinical presentation of التصلب العصيدي and guiding appropriate evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2688
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic factors, indicated by the allowed qualifier, are recognized as relevant contributors to several distinct medical conditions. Specifically, a genetic component is associated with التسمم الوشيقي (viral intoxication), اِضطِراب اللسان (tongue disorder), فَرْطُ الدَّرَقِيَّةِ (hyperthyroidism), and قرحة قلاعية (aphthous ulcer). The presence of a genetic predisposition suggests that hereditary influences may play a role in the development or susceptibility to each of these disorders, underscoring the importance of considering family history and genetic screening in their assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: Other specified conditions related to pregnancy comprise a range of distinct gestational disorders. This category includes renal disorder during pregnancy, peripheral neuropathy during pregnancy, and polymorphic eruption of pregnancy, each representing a separate organ‑system complication that can arise in the context of gestation and is classified under the broader label of other specified pregnancy‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1795
retrieved_docs.content: The skin disorder حُمامَى is linked to other inflammatory conditions of the skin, indicating that it shares pathogenic mechanisms and clinical associations with additional inflammatory dermatoses. This “associated_with” relationship highlights the relevance of recognizing حُمامَى within the broader spectrum of skin inflammation for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: فالج is a general medical condition, and فالج طفلي represents a more specific, narrower form of فالج, indicating the infantile variant of this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2822
retrieved_docs.content: High risk is clinically associated with نزيف مهبلي, which is classified as a noninflammatory disorder of the female genital organs. This relationship indicates that patients identified as high risk may present with or be more likely to develop vaginal bleeding, a condition that falls under the broader category of noninflammatory female genital disorders. Recognizing this association can aid clinicians in monitoring and managing high‑risk individuals for potential vaginal bleeding.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2768
retrieved_docs.content: Nonspecific chest pain, a symptom that lacks a clearly defined cause, has been reported to be clinically associated with oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي). This relationship suggests that patients who present with unexplained chest discomfort may warrant evaluation for an underlying fungal infection of the oral cavity, particularly when other risk factors for candidiasis are present. Recognizing this association can help clinicians adopt a more comprehensive diagnostic approach and ensure appropriate treatment for both the chest pain and any concurrent oral candidiasis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2771
retrieved_docs.content: The allowed qualifier is relevant in the epidemiological context of several disorders, including قَرْحَة مَعِدِيّة (gastric ulcer), intestinal infection, اِضطِراب اللسان (tongue disorder), and تمزق الشبكية (retinal detachment). Recognizing that the allowed qualifier pertains to the epidemiology of these conditions highlights the importance of monitoring their occurrence, distribution, and risk factors within populations, thereby supporting public‑health planning, disease surveillance, and targeted preventive strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2816
retrieved_docs.content: High risk is clinically associated with نزيف مهبلي (vaginal bleeding), indicating that individuals identified as high‑risk may present with vaginal bleeding as a significant clinical manifestation. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as the presence of vaginal bleeding in high‑risk patients can guide diagnostic evaluation, risk stratification, and timely management to address underlying causes and prevent further complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2304

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2848
retrieved_docs.content: حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ and ربط البوق are medical concepts that frequently co‑occur, indicating that the presence of حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ is often associated with the finding or procedure ربط البوق. This consistent association suggests that clinicians should be aware of the link between these two entities, as recognizing one may prompt evaluation for the other, thereby informing diagnosis and guiding appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2460
retrieved_docs.content: التحصّي is associated with other male genital disorders, indicating a connection between this condition and various other disorders affecting the male genitalia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1448
retrieved_docs.content: Nonspecific chest pain is a clinical symptom that can be associated with تضيق الأبهر (aortic stenosis), a cardiac condition characterized by narrowing of the aortic valve and resulting hemodynamic compromise, as well as with داء المبيضات الفموي (oral thrush), an infection of the oral mucosa caused by Candida species. While تضيق الأبهر represents a direct cardiovascular cause of chest discomfort, داء المبيضات الفموي may reflect systemic or immunologic factors that can also manifest with chest pain, highlighting that nonspecific chest pain may arise from both cardiac and non‑cardiac origins. Recognizing this dual association is important for clinicians to conduct a thorough evaluation and to tailor management strategies appropriately.
